_metadata.description: Ingredients backed by clinical research and studies - Enhanced for Section C v3.4.0
changelog: 2.5 (2026-02-20): Added 4 high-impact clinical entries (whey protein, generic citicoline, alpha-GPC, Saccharomyces boulardii strain), upgraded taurine from preclinical to ingredient-human systematic-review evidence, and added brand/generic guardrails for Cognizin aliases. Total entries: 176

2.4 (2026-02-20): Added 8 high-impact clinical records (generic creatine monohydrate, peppermint oil, LGG/BB-12/HN019 strains, GABA, chamomile, beetroot) and aligned 19 systematic-review entries to study_type=systematic_review_meta for score consistency. Total entries: 172

2.3 (2026-02-17): Evidence-quality cleanup pass removed 6 weak/inconclusive entries, renamed PRECLIN_PEA -> INGR_PEA, and downgraded study_type for low-certainty nutrient/herbal records. Total entries: 164
2.2 (2026-02-17): Added 33 clinically reviewed ingredient records (branded, generic, and emerging), expanded elderberry alias coverage for exact-match evidence mapping. Total entries: 170
2.1 (2026-01-05): Added 12 new clinically-backed branded ingredients (Nitrosigine, LJ100, Shoden, Testofen, Magtein, Sunfiber, Cynatine HNS, TamaFlex, HMB, Zynamite, HEAL9). Total entries: 137
2.0 (2025-12-04): Added study_type field support, updated evidence_level mapping for hierarchy scoring

evidence_level_values.product-human: Exact product tested in human RCTs (1.0x multiplier)
evidence_level_values.branded-rct: Branded ingredient with own RCTs (0.8x multiplier) - e.g., KSM-66, Meriva

study_type_values.systematic_review_meta: Meta-analysis or systematic review (6 base pts)
study_type_values.clinical_strain: Probiotic strain clinical evidence (4 base pts)
study_type_values.in_vitro: In vitro/cell studies only (1 base pt)

_metadata._scoring_formula: score = study_type_base_points × evidence_level_multiplier (computed live by scorer, not stored)

_scoring_fields_used.study_type: Determines base points: systematic_review_meta=6, rct_multiple=5, rct_single=4, clinical_strain=4, observational=2, animal_study=2, in_vitro=1
_scoring_fields_used.evidence_level: Determines multiplier: product-human=1.0, branded-rct=0.8, ingredient-human=0.65, strain-clinical=0.6, preclinical=0.3
_scoring_fields_used.score_contribution: Metadata label (tier_1/tier_2/tier_3) reflecting computed score bracket. Not used by scorer.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded curcumin phytosome with explicit human trial evidence in osteoarthritis and psoriasis, plus newer placebo-controlled data in metabolic liver disease.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Belcaro et al. (2010, PMID 21194249) reported improved WOMAC and inflammatory markers with Meriva in osteoarthritis. Antiga et al. (2015, PMID 26090395) randomized psoriasis patients and found adjunctive benefit with oral Meriva. More recent placebo-controlled Meriva data also exist in NASH (PMID 38809154).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: High-concentration full-spectrum ashwagandha root extract with explicit randomized evidence for stress reduction and supportive human data for sleep and quality-of-life outcomes.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Chandrasekhar et al. (2012, PMID 23439798) randomized chronically stressed adults and reported lower stress scores and serum cortisol with a high-concentration full-spectrum root extract commonly mapped to KSM-66. Salve et al. (2020, PMID 32021735) reported additional randomized evidence for stress, anxiety, and sleep-quality improvement with standardized ashwagandha root extract.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Root-and-leaf ashwagandha extract with one clear Sensoril-labeled randomized PubMed trial in knee discomfort and additional composition-matched root-and-leaf stress data; earlier marketing language overstated the branded RCT count.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Tiwari et al. (2016, PMID 27647541) randomized adults with knee joint pain and explicitly used a Sensoril-characterized Withania extract. Broader root-and-leaf stress evidence includes the randomized trial indexed as PMID 38732539, but not every later study is explicitly Sensoril-labeled.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Curcumin-turmeric-oil formulation with explicit branded randomized evidence in knee osteoarthritis; broader mood and inflammatory support exists, but much of that literature is curcumin-level rather than uniquely BCM-95-specific.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Shep et al. (2019, PMID 30975196) compared BCM-95 against diclofenac in knee osteoarthritis and reported similar symptom improvement with fewer GI complaints. Singhal et al. (2021, PMID 33516238) also tested BCM-95 against paracetamol in knee osteoarthritis. Depression evidence exists for curcumin more broadly, but the clearest directly indexed BCM-95 anchors are musculoskeletal.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Highly bioavailable curcumin formulation with explicit randomized evidence in knee osteoarthritis and age-associated memory endpoints.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Nakagawa et al. (2014, PMID 25308211) randomized patients with knee osteoarthritis and found lower pain scores and reduced celecoxib dependence with Theracurmin. Small et al. (2018, PMID 29246725) reported memory benefits and PET biomarker changes in non-demented adults after 18 months of Theracurmin, with later follow-up also reported in PMID 35494290.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: NHS-prescribed multivitamin and mineral product used as a nutritional adjunct in malnutrition, malabsorption, and restricted-diet settings. No PubMed-indexed Forceval-specific clinical trials were identified; support is regulatory and formulary based rather than superiority-trial based.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: No PubMed-indexed Forceval-specific RCTs were identified. The product is documented in the UK electronic medicines compendium Summary of Product Characteristics and in NHS prescribing contexts, so the evidence posture is formulary/regulatory rather than product-trial based.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Retail product using BCM-95 CurcuGreen; the usable evidence is the BCM-95 formulation literature, not PubMed-indexed trials on the finished Life Extension product.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The supporting formulation evidence comes from BCM-95 and CurcuGreen pharmacokinetic or clinical studies such as Antony et al. (2008, PMID 19594223) and later BCM-95 osteoarthritis trials (PMID 30975196; PMID 33516238). No PubMed-indexed trials were identified for the finished Life Extension retail product itself.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded pine-bark extract with explicit randomized evidence in osteoarthritis and supportive meta-analytic evidence for cardiometabolic endpoints.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Belcaro et al. (2008, PMID 18386255) randomized osteoarthritis trial found reduced symptoms and analgesic use with Pycnogenol. Cardiometabolic support is summarized in later meta-analyses including Pourmasoumi et al. (2020, PMID 31637782) and Malekahmadi et al. (2019, PMID 31585179).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Evidence is strongest for the CNCM I-745 yeast strain in antibiotic-associated and acute infectious diarrhea; brand-level claims largely follow that strain literature.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The strain evidence includes a broad review of Saccharomyces boulardii CNCM I-745 (PMID 26316791) and pediatric acute-diarrhea RCT evidence such as the multicenter trial reported in PMID 25653151. Florastor maps to this strain evidence rather than to a distinct product-specific RCT program.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Natural astaxanthin from H. pluvialis with explicit human evidence for skin-aging endpoints; broader exercise and eye-strain claims are supported less directly.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Tominaga et al. (2012, PMID 22428137) reported improved wrinkle and elasticity outcomes with oral/topical astaxanthin from H. pluvialis. AstaReal materials cite later branded skin RCTs including PubMed 31221944, but the strongest directly indexed human anchor remains PMID 22428137.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: One small randomized human trial plus mechanistic follow-up work support reduced post-prandial endotoxemia, but the evidence base remains narrow relative to marketing claims.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: McFarlin et al. (2017, PMID 28868181) randomized 30-day trial in high-fat-meal responders found a 42% reduction in post-prandial endotoxin and a 24% reduction in triglycerides with the commercial spore-based probiotic blend. Human evidence is still limited to this small trial.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Bioactive reduced CoQ10 form with explicit randomized human evidence for endothelial function and strong PK/safety evidence in healthy volunteers.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Sabbatinelli et al. (2020, PMID 32326664) randomized dyslipidemia trial found improved flow-mediated dilation with Kaneka ubiquinol. Hosoe et al. (2007, PMID 16919858) documented dose-dependent absorption and short-term safety up to 300 mg/day in healthy volunteers.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Buffered vitamin C formulation with limited randomized evidence for higher leukocyte vitamin C retention and modest cold-prevention effects; clinical superiority over plain ascorbic acid remains uncertain.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Moyad et al. (2008, PMID 18836692) crossover RCT reported higher leukocyte vitamin C concentrations at 24 hours versus plain ascorbic acid. Van Straten et al. (2002, PMID 12201356) randomized winter trial reported fewer colds and shorter severe-symptom duration, but the overall evidence for meaningful clinical superiority remains limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: No PubMed-indexed human trials were identified for ZyloFresh branded extract. The fallback literature is limited and largely nonhuman, so the entry remains preclinical.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: No PubMed-indexed human trials were identified for ZyloFresh branded extract. Nonhuman alfalfa lipid data include the rabbit study indexed as PMID 22556508, but that does not support a human branded-evidence claim.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: High-purity creatine monohydrate source with published trials explicitly using Creapure in methods, but most efficacy conclusions still reflect the broader creatine-monohydrate literature rather than unique Creapure superiority.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Jagim et al. (2012, PMID 22971354) used Creapure creatine monohydrate as the comparator in a double-blind resistance-training trial and confirmed expected increases in muscle creatine and training adaptation with standard creatine monohydrate dosing. Oliver et al. (2014, PMID 24568653) also used Creapure in a crossover retention study. These support Creapure as a validated source material, while the wider ergogenic evidence remains generic creatine literature.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: The human evidence supports amino-acid-chelated mineral forms such as bisglycinates, but the directly indexed literature is mostly ingredient-form evidence rather than clearly Albion-branded finished-product trials.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Supakatisant et al. (2015, PMID 22909270) randomized pregnant women and found magnesium bisglycinate reduced leg-cramp frequency and intensity versus placebo. Ferrous bisglycinate also has systematic-review support in randomized trials (PMID 36728680). These findings support chelated mineral forms, but not a uniquely PubMed-indexed Albion product-specific RCT set.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Quatrefolic is the glucosamine salt of 5-MTHF with explicit EFSA-reviewed human bioavailability evidence. Most support is PK and biomarker based rather than disease-endpoint RCTs.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: EFSA reviewed human intervention and crossover data for (6S)-5-MTHF glucosamine salt in its 2022 opinion (PMID 36034319) and earlier 2013 safety/bioavailability opinion. Generic 5-MTHF biomarker evidence also includes Venn et al. (2013, PMID 23698160), supporting homocysteine lowering and folate-status improvement.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: European-approved 2 mg prolonged-release melatonin with explicit randomized evidence for sleep quality and low withdrawal risk in older adults.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Lemoine et al. (2007, PMID 18036082) and Wade et al. (2007, PMID 17875243) randomized trials found improved sleep quality and morning alertness with Circadin 2 mg in adults aged 55 and older. Wade et al. (2011, PMID 21091391) reported maintained benefit over 6 months with no rebound or withdrawal signal.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Brand identity remains unverified, so the usable evidence is for lactoferrin as an ingredient rather than for an OptiFerrin-specific product. Human evidence supports iron-status improvement, especially in pregnancy, but not a validated branded ingredient claim here.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Zhao et al. (2022, PMID 35276902) meta-analysis found oral lactoferrin improved ferritin and hemoglobin outcomes versus ferrous sulfate in iron-deficiency anemia trials. Nappi et al. (2009, PMID 19639462) randomized pregnant women with iron-deficiency anemia and reported better tolerability with bovine lactoferrin. No PubMed-indexed RCTs were found for an 'OptiFerrin' brand specifically.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Pharmaceutical-grade L-carnitine tartrate with branded randomized evidence for exercise recovery markers; direct performance gains are less consistent than recovery endpoints.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Volek et al. (2002, PMID 11788381) placebo-controlled crossover trial found 2 g/day L-carnitine L-tartrate reduced biochemical markers of muscle disruption after squat exercise. Ho et al. (2010, PMID 20045157) reported improved recovery markers after 5 weeks of Carnipure tartrate in middle-aged men and women.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: No proprietary SeaKelp product trials were identified; the usable evidence is for kelp-derived iodine supplementation as an ingredient.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Clark et al. (2003, PMID 14583417) double-blind trial in euthyroid adults found dose-dependent urinary iodine increases and higher TSH after kelp supplementation. No branded SeaKelp product has proprietary RCTs; the evidence is for generic kelp/seaweed iodine supplementation only.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports phosphatidylserine as an ingredient, but the cited trials are on soy-derived PS products such as Sharp-PS and Soy-PS rather than on a clearly validated SerinAid-specific trial set.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Vakhapova et al. (2010, PMID 20523044) placebo-controlled trial found PS-DHA improved memory measures in non-demented older adults with memory complaints. Kato-Kataoka et al. (2010, PMID 21103034) reported benefits in lower-baseline-memory subgroups taking soy-derived phosphatidylserine. The evidence is therefore ingredient-level rather than clearly SerinAid-brand-specific.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: No MediHerb-brand-specific randomized trials were identified in PubMed; the usable human evidence is for silymarin as an ingredient, and results are mixed across liver conditions.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Fried et al. (2012, PMID 22797645) multi-center RCT found high-dose silymarin did not improve ALT levels in chronic hepatitis C. Wah Kheong et al. (2017, PMID 28419855) randomized NASH patients to silymarin and reported some histologic improvement signals. This remains ingredient-level rather than MediHerb-brand-specific evidence.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Unique mitochondrial-targeted antioxidant. Randomized human evidence supports vascular-function effects in older adults, with explicit PubMed evidence.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Rossman et al. (2018, PMID 29661838) crossover RCT (n=20, ages 60-79): MitoQ 20 mg/day for 6 weeks improved brachial FMD by about 42% and lowered aortic stiffness in the higher-stiffness subgroup versus placebo. ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT02597023. Larger confirmatory RCTs remain limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded citicoline with explicit PubMed evidence for memory improvement in older adults; additional attentional evidence exists but is less robustly indexed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Nakazaki et al. (2021, PMID 33978188) randomized placebo-controlled trial found Cognizin 500 mg/day improved episodic and composite memory in adults aged 50-85. The study was registered at ClinicalTrials.gov as NCT03369925; earlier small attentional studies provide supportive but less definitive evidence.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: L-theanine product with explicit randomized human evidence for stress-related symptoms and sleep quality.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Hidese et al. (2019, PMID 31623400) crossover RCT found 200 mg/day improved stress-related symptoms and some cognitive measures in healthy adults. Lyon et al. (2011, PMID 22214254) reported improved sleep quality in boys with ADHD using Suntheanine 400 mg/day.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Spore-forming probiotic with explicit randomized human evidence in IBS-D and IBS with depressive symptoms.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Majeed et al. (2016, PMID 26922379) randomized placebo-controlled IBS-D trial found reduced bloating, abdominal pain, and diarrhea with Bacillus coagulans MTCC 5856. Majeed et al. (2018, PMID 29997457) reported improved depressive symptoms and IBS quality-of-life measures in IBS patients with comorbid depression.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Lutein/zeaxanthin blend with explicit randomized human evidence for visual-performance endpoints and large AREDS2 disease-progression evidence.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: AREDS2 Research Group (2013, PMID 23644932) found lutein/zeaxanthin improved AMD outcomes in key subgroups. Hammond et al. (2014, PMID 25468896) randomized 115 adults to lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation and found improved macular pigment, photostress recovery, and glare disability.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Yeast beta-glucan product with explicit randomized evidence for common-cold burden and immune-resilience endpoints.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Auinger et al. (2013, PMID 23340963) multicenter RCT found reduced symptomatic common-cold episodes and better sleep during colds with Wellmune beta-glucan. Additional exercise-stress and URTI studies are supportive, but Auinger et al. remains the clearest PubMed-indexed branded trial.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Patented black pepper extract with human bioavailability studies, but the magnitude and generalizability of its absorption effects remain disputed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Shoba et al. (1998, PMID 9619120) crossover (n=8) reported a large curcumin AUC increase with piperine co-administration, while Badmaev et al. (2000) reported smaller absorption gains for beta-carotene and CoQ10. Fanca-Berthon 2021 raised replication concerns, and Verhoeven 2025 reported no meaningful improvement in free curcumin exposure versus unformulated curcumin.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Reduced glutathione product with randomized human evidence for raising body glutathione stores; cosmetic endpoints have smaller and less clinically important evidence.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Richie et al. (2015, PMID 24791752) RCT (n=54, 6 months): oral glutathione 250 or 1000 mg/day increased blood glutathione stores by about 30-35%, with higher-dose gains in buccal-cell glutathione and NK-cell activity. Weschawalit et al. (2017, PMID 28490897) placebo-controlled trial reported modest skin-related effects, but the stronger evidence remains biomarker-oriented.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Distilled MSM with explicit randomized human evidence in knee pain and osteoarthritis; newer mild-knee-pain data extend support but remain ingredient-like rather than uniquely product-specific.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Kim et al. (2006, PMID 16309928) placebo-controlled osteoarthritis trial found improved pain and physical function with MSM. More recent randomized knee-pain data include Toguchi et al. (2023, PMID 37447322), which showed improved quality-of-life measures after 12 weeks of MSM intake.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Marine multi-mineral complex with a small branded randomized trial in knee osteoarthritis; confirmatory human outcome trials remain limited.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Frestedt et al. (2009, PMID 19187557) randomized placebo-controlled pilot trial found Aquamin improved range of motion and walking distance during NSAID withdrawal in knee osteoarthritis. Evidence remains promising but limited to small studies.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Patented solid lipid formulation; multiple human studies on brain health, mood, and superior absorption compared to standard curcumin.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Cox et al. (2015, PMID 25790012) RCT (n=60, 60-85 yrs): Longvida 400 mg/d for 4 wk improved working memory, sustained attention, reduced fatigue vs placebo (J Psychopharmacol 29:642). Cox et al. (2020, PMID 32157931) RCT (n=80, 50-80 yrs): 12 wk confirmed working memory and fatigue benefits. Earlier marketing claims of dramatically higher free-curcumin bioavailability are not supported by later head-to-head PK data.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Standardized curcuminoid complex with explicit branded rheumatoid-arthritis evidence and broader supportive curcumin literature; very large trial-count claims should be treated cautiously.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Chandran and Goel (2012, PMID 22407780) randomized patients with active rheumatoid arthritis and found symptom improvement with Curcumin C3 Complex. Additional supportive formulation studies exist, but the clearest directly indexed branded anchor is PMID 22407780 rather than a uniformly branded 80-plus-trial evidence base.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Patented low-dose undenatured type II collagen with randomized evidence in knee osteoarthritis and activity-related joint discomfort.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Lugo et al. (2016, PMID 26822714) RCT (n=191, knee OA, 180 days): UC-II 40 mg/day improved WOMAC total, pain, stiffness, and function versus placebo and glucosamine/chondroitin. Crowley et al. (2013, PMID 24153020) placebo-controlled trial in healthy adults with exercise-related joint discomfort reported improved knee extension and longer pain-free exertion with UC-II 40 mg/day.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Hydrolyzed chicken-cartilage matrix with explicit randomized joint-evidence and supportive skin-aging studies.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Schauss et al. (2012, PMID 22486722) randomized osteoarthritis trial found improved pain and WOMAC outcomes with BioCell Collagen. Skin-aging support includes the open-label facial-aging study indexed as PMID 22956862 and later controlled branded work indexed as PMID 31221944.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Patented nicotinamide riboside precursor with randomized human evidence for NAD+ elevation and safety; downstream cardiometabolic benefits remain mixed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Martens et al. (2018, PMID 29599478) crossover RCT (n=24, ages 55-79): nicotinamide riboside 1000 mg/day for 6 weeks raised whole-blood NAD+ by about 60% with exploratory vascular benefits. Dollerup et al. (2018, PMID 29992272) RCT (n=40 obese men): 2000 mg/day for 12 weeks confirmed NAD+ pathway activity and safety but did not improve insulin sensitivity.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded MK-7 product with long-duration randomized human evidence for vascular stiffness and bone-related outcomes in postmenopausal women.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Knapen et al. (2015, PMID 25694037) 3-year RCT (n=244) used 180 mcg/day MenaQ7 and reported improved arterial stiffness and carotid distensibility. Knapen et al. (2013, PMID 23525894) in the same long-term cohort reported improved osteocalcin carboxylation and less femoral-neck bone loss with MK-7 supplementation.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Standardized saffron extract with randomized human evidence for mood and some sleep outcomes in adults and adolescents.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Kell et al. (2017, PMID 28735826) randomized placebo-controlled trial (n=128) found affron 28 mg/day improved mood and stress/anxiety measures over 4 weeks. Lopresti et al. (2018, PMID 29510352) reported improved youth anxiety and depressive symptoms over 8 weeks, and later adult low-mood data are available in PubMed 40414301.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Proprietary magnolia-phellodendron blend with one clear PubMed-indexed randomized stress trial; supporting weight-control and anxiety claims are less robustly indexed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Talbott et al. (2013, PMID 23924268) randomized moderately stressed adults and reported lower salivary cortisol and improved mood-state measures with Relora over 4 weeks. Earlier supportive anxiety and weight-control studies are cited by the brand but are less consistently indexed in primary databases.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Standardized ginkgo extract with one of the largest branded dementia-trial programs among botanical products.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Ihl et al. (2011, PMID 21140383) RCT (n=410, dementia with neuropsychiatric features) found EGb 761 240 mg/day improved cognition, neuropsychiatric symptoms, activities of daily living, and quality of life over 24 weeks. A systematic review/meta-analysis (PMID 25506211) pooled 2,600+ participants and supported benefit with good tolerability.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Patented urolithin A product with randomized human evidence for muscle-endurance and mitochondrial-biomarker effects; not all whole-body performance endpoints are consistently positive.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Singh et al. (2022, PMID 35584623) randomized trial in middle-aged adults reported improved muscle strength, aerobic endurance, and mitochondrial-health biomarkers after 4 months of urolithin A. Andreux et al. (2019, PMID 32694802) first-in-human trial confirmed safety and a molecular signature of improved mitochondrial and cellular health.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Well-studied ergogenic ingredient for high-intensity exercise, with the strongest benefits in efforts lasting roughly 0.5 to 10 minutes.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Saunders et al. (2017, PMID 27797728) meta-analysis of 40 studies found a small overall performance effect and a larger exercise-capacity benefit for efforts lasting about 0.5-10 minutes. Hobson et al. (2012, PMID 22270875) meta-analysis also found the greatest benefit for tasks in the 1-4 minute range.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports citrulline most consistently for exercise-capacity and perceived-exertion endpoints; blood-pressure findings are mixed across meta-analyses, and erectile-function data are mostly combination-product studies rather than standalone citrulline trials.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Rhim et al. (2020, PMID 33308806) meta-analysis found lower perceived exertion and modest exercise-performance benefits with citrulline. Blood-pressure meta-analyses are mixed: PMID 30206378 reported reductions, whereas PMID 30284051 found no clear benefit. Erectile-function support is largely combination-based, such as the PDE5i add-on study indexed as PMID 30150102.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic-review evidence supports hydrolyzed collagen peptides for skin hydration, elasticity, and wrinkles, with newer randomized knee-osteoarthritis studies adding supportive joint-outcome data.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Pu et al. (2023, PMID 37432180) meta-analysis of 26 RCTs found significant improvements in skin hydration and elasticity. More recent syntheses include PMID 40324552 for skin-aging outcomes and PMID 37717022 for osteoarthritis pain, with a newer placebo-controlled knee-OA trial indexed as PMID 39839727.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Oral hyaluronic acid has human evidence for skin hydration and wrinkle-related endpoints, with more limited but supportive literature for osteoarthritis symptoms.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Skin-focused human evidence includes the wrinkle trial indexed as PMID 28761365 and the later placebo-controlled study PMID 34203487. A newer skin meta-analysis is indexed as PMID 40911749. Osteoarthritis support is less consolidated but remains directionally positive across oral-HA trials and reviews.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Meta-analytic human evidence supports garlic most clearly for blood-pressure lowering in hypertensive adults, with additional but smaller lipid-related benefits.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Ried (2020, PMID 32010325) reported clinically meaningful systolic and diastolic blood-pressure reductions with garlic in hypertensive participants. Lipid and metabolic effects are more modest but supported by later pooled analyses such as PMID 37311799.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for MK-7 is strongest for arterial-stiffness biomarkers and osteocalcin carboxylation, with mixed but partly supportive long-term bone-microarchitecture outcomes.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Knapen et al. (2015, PMID 25694037) found less arterial stiffening after 3 years of MK-7 in postmenopausal women. Bone-related trial data include PMID 27625301 and PMID 33030563, which showed favorable effects on osteocalcin carboxylation and some microarchitecture markers, while BMD effects were limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for selenium is strongest in autoimmune thyroid disease, where supplementation can reduce thyroid autoantibody levels in some populations; broader cancer-prevention claims are not a strong supplement-level clinical indication.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Hashimoto-thyroiditis meta-analyses include PMID 38243784 and PMID 37335715, both showing reductions in thyroid autoantibodies in some trial settings. Earlier evidence is summarized in PMID 20883174 and PMID 27702392. Benefits outside specific deficiency or thyroid-autoimmunity contexts are less clinically consistent.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human trials for depression, fibromyalgia, and weight loss via serotonin modulation.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Maffei (2020) systematic review & meta-analysis: 5-HTP depression remission rate 0.65 (95% CI 0.55-0.78), large effect (Hedges' g=1.11). Shaw et al. (2002) Cochrane-style analysis: tryptophan/5-HTP better than placebo (Peto OR 4.1); limited by small samples and publication bias.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports SAMe for depression, especially as adjunctive therapy, and older randomized osteoarthritis studies suggest symptom relief comparable to NSAIDs with fewer adverse effects.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent depression meta-analyses include PMID 38423354 and PMID 38199136, both supporting antidepressant benefit with the strongest signal in adjunctive use. Older osteoarthritis evidence remains supportive but dates back to earlier randomized comparisons summarized in the classic review literature.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Extensive human evidence supports caffeine for acute exercise performance and vigilance-related cognitive tasks, while broader body-composition claims depend heavily on context and total diet.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Exercise-performance evidence is summarized in the umbrella review PMID 30926628 and resistance-training meta-analysis PMID 32551869. Cognitive-task effects during sport and exertion are summarized in PMID 33800853. Acute placebo-controlled trials such as PMID 34139220 also support improvements in running performance and cognition.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports berberine most clearly for glycemic and lipid outcomes in metabolic-risk populations, with weight effects present but generally modest.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent pooled evidence includes PMID 39599040, which found consistent improvements in fasting glucose, HbA1c, and blood lipids with berberine supplementation. Earlier meta-analytic support is also indexed as PMID 36044031. The strongest signal is metabolic rather than dramatic weight loss.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Cocoa flavanol with studies in muscle growth, cardiovascular health, exercise performance.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Hooper et al. (2012) Cochrane meta-analysis (20 RCTs, n=856): chocolate/cocoa improved FMD +1.45% and reduced DBP; doses >50 mg epicatechin/d showed greater BP effects. Mastroiacovo et al. (2015) RCT (n=90, elderly): cocoa flavanols improved cognitive performance dose-dependently.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports standardized Rhodiola rosea extracts for stress-related fatigue and some cognitive-performance endpoints, but effect sizes vary across extract types and standardization schemes.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Olsson et al. (2009, PMID 20374970) reported reduced stress-related fatigue and better attention after 28 days of SHR-5 Rhodiola. A broader systematic review is indexed as PMID 23420780 and concluded that Rhodiola shows promise, while also noting trial heterogeneity and variable extract standardization.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Direct human evidence specific to magnesium bisglycinate is limited. Small trials suggest good tolerability and possible benefit for leg cramps or sleep-related outcomes, while the larger evidence base is for magnesium more generally rather than the glycinate form alone.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Direct bisglycinate evidence includes the leg-cramp trial indexed as PMID 22909270. Broader magnesium-sleep syntheses such as PMID 34811585 suggest possible benefit in selected populations, but they are not specific to glycinate and the effect size remains modest.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic-review evidence supports NAC most clearly for chronic respiratory indications and as a glutathione precursor, with growing but more heterogeneous psychiatric-adjunct data.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Respiratory evidence is synthesized in PMID 37171007, which supports NAC in chronic bronchitis and COPD-type settings. Psychiatric-adjunct evidence is summarized in more recent reviews including PMID 38008060, though effect sizes vary by disorder and background treatment.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for quercetin is strongest for modest blood-pressure and inflammatory-marker effects. Immune and antiviral claims are studied frequently, but often in combination regimens that make standalone attribution difficult.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Serban et al. (2016, PMID 27153731) found modest reductions in systolic and diastolic blood pressure with quercetin supplementation, especially at higher doses. Later review literature in PubMed continues to support anti-inflammatory and cardiometabolic effects, while direct antiviral conclusions remain less secure because many studies use combination protocols.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports L-theanine for subjective stress reduction and selected attention-related outcomes, especially in combination with caffeine or in acute stress paradigms.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent meta-analytic evidence includes PMID 38196056, which supports stress-reduction benefits, and PMID 39776999, which found mixed but partly positive effects on attention and processing-speed endpoints depending on dose and task.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports Bacopa monnieri for memory-related outcomes, particularly after longer supplementation periods, though not every cognitive domain improves consistently.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Kongkeaw et al. (2016, PMID 26745481) meta-analysis found improvements in attention and cognitive processing after chronic Bacopa supplementation. More recent review literature, including PMID 31415365, supports memory-related benefits while noting variability across extracts and study populations.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for CoQ10 is strongest in heart-failure and cardiometabolic settings, with additional support for statin-associated muscle symptoms in some trials. The superiority of ubiquinone versus ubiquinol is not settled uniformly across use cases.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent heart-failure meta-analytic support includes PMID 38735037. The landmark Q-SYMBIO randomized trial is indexed as PMID 25282031 and reported lower major adverse cardiovascular events with CoQ10 in chronic heart failure. Broader mitochondrial or ATP-language should be treated as mechanistic support rather than a universally demonstrated clinical endpoint.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Extensive systematic review evidence supports omega-3 for cardiovascular health, triglyceride reduction, and mood. High-dose EPA particularly effective for CV risk reduction. Quality and formulation affect bioavailability.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Hu et al. (2019) JAMA Cardiology meta-analysis (13 RCTs, n=127,477): marine omega-3 supplementation reduced CHD death (RR 0.92), MI (RR 0.87), and total CHD events (RR 0.93). Abdelhamid et al. (2020) Cochrane (86 RCTs): long-chain omega-3 slightly reduced CHD mortality and events.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Extensive evidence from systematic reviews and meta-analyses (2024-2025) supports vitamin D3 for bone health, immune function, and mood. 2024 Endocrine Society guidelines recommend empiric supplementation for specific populations.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Bolland et al. (2018) Lancet meta-analysis (81 RCTs, n=53,537): vitamin D did not prevent fractures or falls or improve BMD. Martineau et al. (2017) Cochrane (25 RCTs, n=11,321): vitamin D reduced acute respiratory infections (OR 0.88); benefit greatest in deficient individuals.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports zinc most clearly for common-cold treatment when delivered as high-dose lozenges started early. That evidence applies mainly to acetate or gluconate lozenges and should not be over-attributed to zinc picolinate specifically.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: High-dose zinc-lozenge meta-analyses include PMID 25888289, PMID 27378206, and PMID 28515951, which reported shorter cold duration when treatment began within 24 hours. These studies were based on acetate or gluconate lozenges rather than zinc picolinate-specific trials.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports lutein most clearly for increasing macular pigment optical density and selected visual-performance endpoints, often in combination with zeaxanthin rather than as an isolated lutein-only intervention.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent pooled human evidence includes PMID 41573009, which found better macular pigment and visual-sensitivity outcomes in myopia studies using lutein plus zeaxanthin. Broader visual-performance evidence also includes trials and meta-analyses of lutein-zeaxanthin combinations such as PMID 28661438.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Evidence for biotin supplementation is strongest in deficiency-related hair or nail problems. High-quality placebo-controlled evidence in non-deficient individuals remains limited, and many recent trials use combination products rather than standalone biotin.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent combination-product evidence includes the randomized trial indexed as PMID 40896024, while observational postoperative hair-loss data include PMID 32762597. Overall, PubMed support for standalone biotin in non-deficient adults remains limited compared with its clear role in correcting deficiency.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Strong systematic-review evidence supports melatonin for sleep-onset latency and circadian-rhythm or jet-lag use cases, with the clearest benefits in people with sleep-initiation difficulty or misaligned timing.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A recent dose-response meta-analysis is indexed as PMID 38888087 and found reduced sleep-onset latency with effects peaking around 4 mg/day. Earlier jet-lag and circadian-use evidence is summarized in classic systematic reviews such as PMID 12076414, and prolonged-release insomnia evidence is covered separately in the Circadin entry.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for Panax ginseng is strongest for fatigue-related outcomes, with more mixed but still supportive data for selected cognitive and metabolic endpoints depending on extract type and population.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Fatigue-focused syntheses include PMID 29624410 and PMID 35776997, both supporting modest benefit with acceptable safety. Additional metabolic and cardiometabolic evidence is summarized in PMID 35509826, but results vary across preparations and indications.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports Rhodiola rosea for stress-related fatigue and some endurance or exertion outcomes, but effect estimates vary meaningfully by extract standardization and study design.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent endurance-performance meta-analysis PMID 41080184 found modest improvements in endurance-related outcomes and biomarkers. Earlier systematic-review evidence, including PMID 23420780, concluded that Rhodiola shows promise for fatigue and performance but that trial quality and extract heterogeneity remain important limitations.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Vitamin B12 clearly benefits deficiency correction and neurologic or hematologic protection in deficient states. In people without overt deficiency, human trial evidence for cognition or fatigue improvement is much weaker.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Randomized-trial synthesis PMID 33809274 found no consistent cognitive or depressive benefit from B12 supplementation in people without overt deficiency. NIH ODS also emphasizes B12's established role in deficiency correction and neurologic health rather than general nootropic benefit in replete adults.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports some Lactobacillus- and Bifidobacterium-based probiotics for IBS and related gut outcomes, but benefits are strain-specific and should not be generalized to all probiotic blends.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent IBS-focused syntheses include PMID 37772692, PMID 38999862, and the strain-specific meta-analysis PMID 41682832, which found benefit for some Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains but not for all probiotics uniformly. This entry therefore reflects class-level support with strong strain dependence.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic-review evidence supports curcumin for lowering inflammatory markers and improving osteoarthritis pain, but effect sizes vary by formulation and bioavailability remains a major limitation for generic turmeric products.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent meta-analyses include PMID 38945354 and PMID 38576215, both reporting reductions in CRP and related inflammatory markers with curcumin. Osteoarthritis pain support is also summarized in PMID 40615851. Stronger outcomes tend to come from enhanced formulations rather than plain turmeric powder.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Vitamin C clearly supports deficiency correction, antioxidant biology, and collagen synthesis. For common-cold outcomes, the best human evidence supports modest shortening of duration rather than strong prevention effects in the general population.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The major cold-literature synthesis remains Hemila and Chalker (PMID 23440782), which found shorter cold duration but limited prevention in the general population. Later review updates include PMID 38082300, while high-exertion prevention effects were highlighted in earlier analyses such as PMID 17636648.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports ginger most clearly for nausea-related indications, especially pregnancy-associated nausea, with additional but smaller anti-inflammatory and pain-related benefits.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Pregnancy-nausea evidence is synthesized in PMID 24642205 and PMID 21818642, both supporting ginger in common dose ranges. Anti-inflammatory-marker reductions were summarized by Jalali et al. (2020, PMID 31937153). Evidence outside nausea is supportive but less consistent.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports ashwagandha for stress and anxiety reduction, with moderate improvements in perceived-stress and cortisol outcomes across randomized trials. Effect sizes vary by extract, dose, and study duration.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent meta-analyses include PMID 39348746 and PMID 36017529, both supporting lower perceived stress and cortisol with ashwagandha. Newer trials continue to be published, but heterogeneity across branded extracts means generic claims should stay moderate rather than absolute.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports green-tea catechins for small reductions in body weight, body fat, and some oxidative-stress markers, especially when caffeine is present. Effects are generally modest rather than transformative.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent GRADE-based pooled evidence includes PMID 38031409, which found small but statistically significant benefits for body mass, BMI, and oxidative-stress endpoints. Earlier catechin-plus-caffeine evidence is captured in PMID 19597519 and remains directionally supportive for fat oxidation, though clinical effect sizes are modest.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Clinical studies support liver protection and enzyme normalization.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Kalopitas et al. (2024) meta-analysis (26 RCTs, n=2,375 NAFLD/NASH): silymarin reduced ALT, AST, TC, TG, LDL-C, fasting insulin; improved steatosis on histology. Earlier Cochrane reviews found no benefit in viral hepatitis/cirrhosis mortality.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for lion's mane is promising but still limited. Small randomized trials suggest benefit in mild cognitive impairment and mood-related outcomes, while mechanistic nerve-growth claims remain more preclinical than clinically confirmed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The classic mild-cognitive-impairment trial is Mori et al. (2009, PMID 18844328). More recent review literature includes PMID 38140277 and PMID 40276537, both concluding that the human signal is encouraging but still based on small, heterogeneous studies.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Small human trials suggest glycine may improve subjective sleep quality and next-day fatigue, but the evidence base is still limited and much smaller than for mainstream sleep agents.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Small crossover and sleep-restriction studies include PMID 22529837 and PMID 25533534, both suggesting better subjective sleep quality or less next-day fatigue with bedtime glycine. A recent review is indexed as PMID 37851316 and notes that the evidence remains limited by sample size and study count.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for tart cherry is stronger for exercise recovery and inflammation than for sleep. Sleep-related benefits are possible, but the trial base is small and results are mixed.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recovery and soreness evidence includes systematic reviews such as PMID 35790450 and the earlier sleep-focused pilot trial PMID 22038497. More recent sleep-review literature, including PMID 40964149, suggests potential benefit but emphasizes limited sample sizes and mixed study quality.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for elderberry in upper-respiratory illness is promising but still limited by small trial counts and heterogeneous preparations. The data do not justify strong antiviral or oseltamivir-equivalence claims.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The 2021 review indexed as PMID 33827515 found limited evidence for symptom-duration reduction without support for cytokine-storm concerns. Earlier pooled respiratory-symptom evidence is indexed as PMID 30670267. Overall, the trial base remains small and formulation-specific.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports fenugreek most consistently for glycemic outcomes, with smaller and more variable support for testosterone- or libido-related endpoints in men.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Testosterone-related evidence is summarized in PMID 32048383, which found modest androgen-related effects in men. Glycemic support remains stronger and is captured in the earlier metabolic meta-analysis PMID 25023485 and later pooled work on glucose-control outcomes.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for bitter melon remains mixed. Some small trials suggest glycemic benefit, but pooled analyses consistently note heterogeneous preparations and no robust overall effect.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic reviews including PMID 31084904 and PMID 37626876 concluded that Momordica charantia has heterogeneous and generally low-certainty evidence for glucose control. The overall signal is not strong enough to present bitter melon as a reliable standalone glycemic intervention.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for alpha-lipoic acid is strongest in diabetic-neuropathy symptom relief, with additional support for modest improvements in glucose-control and oxidative-stress markers.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The diabetic-neuropathy RCT anchor remains SYDNEY 2 (PMID 17065691). More recent pooled evidence for metabolic markers includes PMID 30019119 and related meta-analyses, which show modest improvements in glycemic control and oxidative-stress parameters.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: FDA-approved heart health claim; extensive evidence for cholesterol reduction.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Abumweis et al. (2008) meta-analysis (59 RCTs): plant sterols/stanols reduced LDL-C 0.31 mmol/L (8.8%); effect continued up to 3 g/d (~12% reduction). Scholle et al. (2016) meta-analysis: additionally lowered TC and LDL-C in statin-treated patients beyond monotherapy.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence strongly supports red yeast rice for LDL-cholesterol lowering, largely via monacolin K. Because monacolin K is pharmacologically similar to lovastatin, efficacy and safety interpretation should reflect that statin-like mechanism.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent pooled lipid-lowering evidence includes PMID 35264949 and PMID 33497775, both supporting meaningful LDL reductions with red yeast rice. The key mechanism is monacolin K, which is structurally identical to lovastatin, so efficacy and adverse-effect interpretation should remain statin-aware.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports prescription-grade crystalline glucosamine sulfate for osteoarthritis pain and possibly structural progression, but results are more mixed for other glucosamine forms.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The umbrella review PMID 32827334 and the meta-analysis PMID 30046955 both support crystalline glucosamine sulfate over hydrochloride forms for osteoarthritis outcomes. The evidence is strongest for prescription-grade sulfate preparations rather than generic glucosamine broadly.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Clinical evidence for joint health; often combined with glucosamine.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Singh et al. (2015) Cochrane meta-analysis (43 RCTs, n=9,110): chondroitin 800-1200 mg/d reduced OA pain (SMD -0.63) and improved function (SMD -0.82). Kahan et al. (2009) structural meta-analysis (3 RCTs, 2-yr): small but significant structure-modifying effect on joint space narrowing.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Multiple RCTs for urinary tract health and UTI prevention.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Williams et al. (2023) Cochrane (50 RCTs, n=8,857): cranberry reduced UTI risk 30% (RR 0.70, moderate certainty); women with recurrent UTIs and children benefited most. PACs inhibit P-fimbriated E. coli adhesion to urothelial cells; ≥36 mg PACs/d recommended.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Meta-analytic human evidence supports cinnamon for modest fasting-glucose and insulin-resistance improvements, but HbA1c and long-term glycemic effects are less consistently robust across trials.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent pooled evidence includes PMID 38218735, which supports fasting-glucose and HOMA-IR improvements with cinnamon supplementation. Earlier meta-analysis PMID 24019277 also found favorable glycemic and lipid shifts, although the effect size on HbA1c remained modest.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for digestive enzymes is strong in exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, but evidence for broad over-the-counter digestive support in otherwise healthy adults remains limited and product-specific.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Pancreatic-enzyme replacement evidence is well established in pancreatic insufficiency and summarized in guidance and reviews such as PMID 33931040. Over-the-counter multi-enzyme blends have much less robust support; one placebo-controlled dyspepsia trial is indexed as PMID 37976892, but no broad general-population meta-analysis exists.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports inulin-type fructans most clearly for bifidogenic effects and laxation-related outcomes, with additional but more variable evidence for mineral absorption and cardiometabolic endpoints.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent systematic reviews include PMID 35833477 and PMID 34555168, both supporting consistent increases in Bifidobacterium and favorable bowel-function effects with inulin-type fructans. Cardiometabolic effects are smaller and less uniform, as reflected in PMID 38309832.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports ferrous bisglycinate as a better-tolerated oral iron option, with the clearest comparative benefit in pregnancy and other deficiency-risk groups.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The main comparative synthesis is PMID 36728680, which found higher hemoglobin and fewer gastrointestinal adverse events with ferrous bisglycinate versus other oral irons in pregnancy. Benefits outside pregnancy are plausible but supported by fewer direct head-to-head trials.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports 5-MTHF as a bioavailable active folate form that performs at least comparably to folic acid while generating less unmetabolized folic acid in some settings. The strongest support is pharmacokinetic and biomarker-based rather than hard clinical-endpoint superiority.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Randomized and comparative evidence includes PMID 31425504, PMID 16600937, and PMID 16151461, all supporting 5-MTHF as at least as effective as folic acid for maintaining folate status. Additional unmetabolized-folic-acid context comes from PMID 25943647 and PMID 25733468.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for valerian is mixed but suggests possible improvement in subjective sleep quality and associated anxiety symptoms. Objective sleep-metric benefits are less consistent across trials.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The most complete recent synthesis is PMID 33086877, which found improvement in subjective sleep quality and some anxiety-related outcomes but substantial heterogeneity. Earlier meta-analytic evidence, including Bent et al., remains directionally similar.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for passionflower suggests anxiolytic and mild sleep-quality benefits, but the trial base is relatively small and methodologically heterogeneous.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The broad neuropsychiatric review is PMID 33352740, which concluded that most small trials reported reduced anxiety with Passiflora incarnata. Sleep-specific randomized evidence includes PMID 21294203, which found better subjective sleep quality with passionflower tea in healthy adults.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports lemon balm for acute anxiety or mood-calming effects, with some additional cognitive findings in small trials. The overall evidence base remains modest and somewhat heterogeneous.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The best pooled evidence is PMID 34449930, which found significant reductions in anxiety and depression scores with lemon balm across small clinical trials. Individual randomized studies such as the Cases trial remain supportive but not definitive.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Fisetin remains primarily preclinical. Human data are limited to a small uncontrolled pilot with mixed biological-age results (PMID 39269340), while the main placebo-controlled efficacy studies are still ongoing on ClinicalTrials.gov.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A small pilot in healthy older adults (PMID 39269340) did not show a consistent anti-aging signal and had no control arm. No completed placebo-controlled fisetin efficacy trial is published yet; ongoing randomized studies include AFFIRM-LITE (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03675724) and COVID-FISETIN (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT04476953).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Induces autophagy and extends lifespan in yeast, flies, and mice; mechanistic rationale for aging.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Wirth et al. (2018, PMID 30388439) phase IIa DB-RCT (n=30) reported positive memory effects in subjective cognitive decline; Schwarz et al. (2022, PMID 35616942) DB-RCT (n=37) confirmed safety with modest biomarker changes.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: AKG remains predominantly preclinical. Published human evidence is not yet outcome-driven; the strongest rigorous human work is the ongoing placebo-controlled ABLE trial framework rather than completed efficacy data (PMID 37217632, PMID 40819772).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The published human literature is centered on the ABLE placebo-controlled trial protocol (PMID 37217632) and its recruitment feasibility report (PMID 40819772). Completed randomized efficacy outcomes for Ca-AKG have not yet been published, so longevity or metabolic claims should remain conservative.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Inhibits CD38 enzyme to boost NAD+; preclinical data for sleep and anti-aging effects.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Chamomile extract (1.2% apigenin) DB-RCT showed GAD symptom reduction (PMID 19593179), but no dedicated apigenin-alone RCTs exist. CD38 inhibition mechanism for NAD+ is supported preclinically.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Stabilizes mast cells and reduces neuroinflammation in models; potential for allergies and brain health.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: DB-RCT of PEA+luteolin improved post-COVID olfactory recovery (PMID 35450527); open-label ASD work with luteolin+quercetin reported GI improvement (PMID 22492777). No standalone luteolin RCTs were identified.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human randomized and meta-analytic evidence supports taurine for modest improvements in blood pressure and selected cardiometabolic markers; effects vary by dose and baseline risk.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analysis of RCTs reported blood-pressure and lipid improvements with taurine supplementation (PMID: 32871172). A newer 2025 meta-analysis reported broader cardiometabolic improvements across 34 RCTs (PMID: 41275513).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports quercetin phytosome as a better-absorbed formulation than quercetin aglycone, with a small number of formulation-specific clinical trials. Outcome evidence is still limited and indication-specific rather than broad or definitive.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A human intervention meta-analysis found formulation strongly influences circulating quercetin exposure, with phytosome-based products among the most bioavailable options (PMID 40037045). A placebo-controlled seasonal-allergy trial using Quercetin Phytosome improved symptom scores (PMID 35776034), while an open-label randomized COVID-19 study used Quercetin Phytosome but remains too disease-specific to treat as broad efficacy evidence (PMID 36712674).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for NADH is real but modest. A small crossover RCT suggested benefit in chronic fatigue syndrome, while larger modern trials mostly test NADH in combination with CoQ10 rather than as a strong standalone intervention (PMID 10071523, PMID 26212172, PMID 34444817).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A crossover pilot in chronic fatigue syndrome found 8/26 responders on NADH versus 2/26 on placebo (PMID 10071523). Combination trials of CoQ10 plus NADH also improved selected fatigue-related outcomes in ME/CFS, including randomized studies with exercise and symptom endpoints (PMID 26212172; PMID 34444817), but direct NADH-alone evidence remains limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Multiple human exercise RCTs showing body composition and performance benefits at 2-2.5g/day. Also established as methyl donor for homocysteine reduction (FDA-approved for homocystinuria). Previously misclassified as preclinical.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Multiple human RCTs: 14-wk DB trial in soccer players (2g/d) strength/power gains; 6-wk trial improved body composition in trained men (Cholewa 2018, JISSN).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human meta-analytic evidence supports generic astaxanthin for modest improvements in exercise-related fatigue/efficiency, oxidative-stress biomarkers, and some skin-aging endpoints. Cardiometabolic effects are less consistent, and branded preparations still have the stronger individual trial base.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A 2024 meta-analysis of 11 RCTs found improvements in fatigue, motor function, and exercise efficiency (PMID 38243785). Additional meta-analyses support reductions in oxidative-stress and inflammation biomarkers (PMID 35091276) and improvements in skin-aging measures (PMID 34578794), whereas cardiometabolic outcomes are more mixed despite some favorable pooled signals (PMID 32755613).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Sulforaphane now has meaningful human trial evidence across autism, former-smoker chemoprevention biomarkers, prediabetes, and inflammatory settings, but effect sizes vary by indication, formulation, and baseline responder profile. Broccoli sprout preparations remain the main clinical source.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A randomized phase II trial in former smokers found significant reduction in bronchial Ki-67 labeling with sulforaphane versus placebo (PMID 40041932). Autism evidence now includes a 2025 meta-analysis of six RCTs with selective symptom improvement but notable heterogeneity (PMID 40458076), alongside individual randomized trials such as PMIDs 32347624, 34034808, and 36427174. In prediabetes, a 12-week placebo-controlled trial showed only a small overall fasting-glucose reduction with responder heterogeneity (PMID 39929977).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for pterostilbene exists but remains limited. The main randomized trial suggests possible blood-pressure benefit and acceptable short-term tolerability, but the same program also raised concern for LDL increases with monotherapy (PMID 25057276, PMID 23431291).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: In a randomized placebo-controlled trial in hypercholesterolemic adults, pterostilbene lowered systolic and diastolic blood pressure in some analyses but increased LDL in the monotherapy arm (PMID 25057276). A companion safety analysis from the same clinical program found doses up to 250 mg/day generally well tolerated over 6-8 weeks (PMID 23431291).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Artichoke extract has real human evidence for functional dyspepsia and modest lipid lowering. The evidence base is materially stronger than preclinical-only status, though the benefits are moderate rather than drug-like.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A multicenter placebo-controlled trial in 247 patients with functional dyspepsia reported significant symptom improvement with artichoke leaf extract (PMID 14653829). For lipids, randomized evidence includes individual placebo-controlled studies (PMID 18424099; PMID 22746542) and pooled meta-analyses showing modest reductions in total and LDL cholesterol (PMID 28609140; PMID 34569671).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for deglycyrrhizinated licorice is modest but real. The strongest modern trials are on the standardized GutGard extract for functional dyspepsia, H. pylori management, and reflux-related symptoms, while older classic DGL ulcer trials were mixed or negative (PMID 21747893, PMID 23606875, PMID 39929150, PMID 4933135).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: GutGard placebo-controlled trials showed symptom improvement in functional dyspepsia (PMID 21747893), higher H. pylori clearance than placebo in a 60-day study (PMID 23606875), and faster relief of reflux-related symptoms in a larger 2025 trial (PMID 39929150). By contrast, a classic randomized DGL duodenal-ulcer trial found no advantage over placebo (PMID 4933135), so this entry should stay evidence-cautious.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports zinc carnosine/polaprezinc for gastric mucosal healing and as an adjunct in H. pylori or ulcer settings, but much of the literature is adjunctive, Asia-centered, or comparator-controlled rather than placebo-rich. The overall evidence is clearly beyond preclinical, though not as broad as generic 'gut barrier' marketing claims often imply.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A multicenter randomized trial in gastric-ulcer patients found polaprezinc comparable to rebamipide after 8 weeks, with endoscopic healing rates of 81.5% versus 74.3% (PMID 35999163). In H. pylori-associated gastritis, adjunctive polaprezinc improved eradication rates in a multicenter randomized study (PMID 28407007), and a 2022 meta-analysis supported that adjunctive signal (PMID 36235778). Additional randomized human studies include aspirin-related small-bowel injury (PMID 23826914) and ulcerative colitis enemas (PMID 24286534).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports Boswellia serrata for osteoarthritis symptom relief, especially pain and stiffness, with pooled RCT support and newer placebo-controlled branded-extract trials. Most of the strongest data are joint-health specific rather than broad anti-inflammatory benefit across conditions (PMID 32680575, PMID 39092235).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A meta-analysis of seven randomized trials found significant improvements in WOMAC pain, stiffness, and function in osteoarthritis (PMID 32680575). More recent placebo-controlled trials with standardized extracts also reported relatively rapid symptomatic improvement, including the 2024 Boswellin Super study (PMID 39092235) and the earlier Aflapin trial (PMID 35512759).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for huperzine A is mixed. Meta-analyses report cognitive benefits in Alzheimer's disease and mild cognitive impairment, but many underlying studies have high risk of bias, and the best-known U.S. phase II Alzheimer trial missed its primary endpoint (PMID 24086396, PMID 21502597, PMID 30989926).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: The largest synthesis in Alzheimer's disease pooled 20 RCTs and found apparent MMSE and ADL benefits, but also emphasized poor methodological quality (PMID 24086396). The multicenter U.S. phase II Alzheimer trial found no benefit on its primary ADAS-Cog endpoint at 200 mcg twice daily (PMID 21502597). Additional meta-analytic support exists in vascular dementia and MCI populations (PMID 24639880; PMID 30989926), but the evidence base remains quality-limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for Cordyceps is modest and mostly exercise-related. Small randomized trials suggest improvements in ventilatory or metabolic thresholds and high-intensity exercise tolerance, but they do not establish large or consistent VO2max gains across populations (PMID 20804368, PMID 27408987, PMID 38501161).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: In healthy older adults, a placebo-controlled trial of Cs-4 improved metabolic and ventilatory thresholds after 12 weeks but not VO2max itself (PMID 20804368). A later randomized study of Cordyceps militaris found better tolerance to high-intensity exercise after acute and chronic supplementation (PMID 27408987). A newer human study also reported altered skeletal-muscle stem-cell recruitment after interval exercise (PMID 38501161), but the broader performance evidence remains limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Betaine HCl has human physiological evidence for transient gastric re-acidification under drug-induced hypochlorhydria, but direct symptom-outcome trials for dyspepsia, reflux, or protein digestion are still lacking. It should not be presented as purely preclinical, yet the human evidence remains narrow and mechanistic (PMID 23980906, PMID 25274610).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A pilot human study showed that 1500 mg betaine HCl rapidly lowered gastric pH in rabeprazole-induced hypochlorhydria (PMID 23980906). A randomized crossover study then demonstrated that this re-acidification restored dasatinib exposure under hypochlorhydric conditions (PMID 25274610). These are meaningful human mechanism studies, but not direct digestive-symptom efficacy trials.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for saffron is strongest in mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms, where multiple meta-analyses show benefit versus placebo and no clear inferiority versus SSRIs. The evidence is still concentrated in a relatively small and geographically clustered trial base, so the signal is promising rather than definitive (PMID 31135916, PMID 38913392, PMID 41693488).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses support saffron for depressive symptoms compared with placebo (PMID 31135916) and show no clear efficacy difference versus SSRIs across eight comparative trials (PMID 38913392). A newer GRADE-assessed 2025 meta-analysis of 34 RCTs confirmed benefit on self-reported depression and anxiety scales, while also showing that clinician-rated scales are less consistently improved (PMID 41693488).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for chromium picolinate is mixed but clearly beyond preclinical status. Meta-analyses in type 2 diabetes show statistically significant glycemic improvements on average, yet heterogeneity is high and at-risk or prediabetic populations often do not benefit in better-controlled trials (PMID 32730903, PMID 20634174, PMID 17519436).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A 2020 meta-analysis of 28 randomized trials in type 2 diabetes reported reductions in fasting glucose, HbA1c, insulin, and HOMA-IR with chromium supplementation (PMID 32730903). However, a placebo-controlled trial in adults at risk for diabetes found no improvement in glucose tolerance or insulin sensitivity with 500-1000 mcg/day chromium picolinate (PMID 20634174). Older systematic evidence also found more consistent benefit in diabetes than in non-diabetic populations, while highlighting study-quality concerns (PMID 17519436).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Cochrane review and meta-analyses confirm acetyl-L-carnitine efficacy for peripheral neuropathic pain (20.2% reduction vs placebo). Established mitochondrial support mechanism. 6-12 month treatment duration needed. Previously misclassified as preclinical.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analysis of 4 RCTs (n=523): 20.2% pain reduction in diabetic/HIV neuropathy (P<0.0001); Cochrane review confirms benefit at 6-12 months, Li et al. PLoS ONE.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for NMN is real but still early. Meta-analyses confirm that oral NMN reliably increases circulating NAD-related markers, while most clinical outcomes remain small, heterogeneous, or not clearly different from placebo across short trials (PMID 39116016, PMID 39531138, PMID 39185644).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analyses of randomized trials conclude that NMN elevates blood NAD levels but does not consistently improve glucose or lipid endpoints (PMID 39116016; PMID 39531138). A 12-week placebo-controlled study in older adults reported higher NAD, maintained walking speed, and better sleep-quality subscales (PMID 38789831), while other randomized trials have shown mixed or null functional findings, including in older adults with diabetes (PMID 36443648) and in a separate 12-week older-adult study (PMID 35215405).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for PQQ is promising but still limited to small randomized trials and combination-formula studies. The strongest signal is in short-term cognition-related outcomes and exploratory brain-metabolism markers rather than large, independently replicated clinical endpoints (PMID 34415830, PMID 38908296).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A 12-week placebo-controlled trial in middle-aged and older adults reported improvements in several Cognitrax domains with 21.5 mg/day PQQ disodium salt (PMID 34415830). A later 6-week randomized trial in mild cognitive impairment found promising brain-metabolism and orientation changes with a dihydrogen-plus-PQQ mixture, but the design was small and combination-based rather than pure PQQ monotherapy (PMID 38908296).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Resveratrol has a broad human trial base, but the overall evidence remains mixed and outcome-specific. Some meta-analyses find modest improvements in inflammatory or lipid biomarkers, while others show no consistent clinically meaningful effect, so longevity-style claims remain substantially ahead of the data (PMID 39872318, PMID 35905799, PMID 39255906, PMID 29305228).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent meta-analyses report reduced inflammatory and oxidative-stress biomarkers in type 2 diabetes (PMID 39872318) and lower CRP/TNF-alpha in cardiovascular populations (PMID 35905799). Lipid findings are inconsistent: some meta-analyses report dose-dependent LDL/TG reductions (PMID 36145131; PMID 39255906), whereas others find no meaningful lipid benefit overall (PMID 29305228; PMID 24111838).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Curcumin has one of the largest human supplement trial literatures, with broad meta-analytic support for inflammatory, osteoarthritis, and some metabolic endpoints. However, formulation quality and bioavailability matter materially, and the strongest effects are generally seen with enhanced-delivery products rather than unformulated spice-level curcumin (PMID 39478418, PMID 38945354, PMID 38576215, PMID 40615851).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A comprehensive 103-trial meta-analysis found high-credibility evidence for improvements in fasting glucose, CRP, HDL, and body weight, with significant effects across 23 of 42 pooled outcomes (PMID 39478418). Additional umbrella-style work supports anti-inflammatory and endothelial-function biomarker effects (PMID 38945354) and osteoarthritis symptom benefit (PMID 38576215), while newer disease-specific syntheses continue to show reductions in CRP and TNF-alpha in knee osteoarthritis (PMID 40615851).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for berberine is strong in metabolic-health settings, especially for glycemic control and selected lipid outcomes, but much of the literature is short-term, China-centered, and methodologically uneven. The overall signal is still clearly beyond preclinical status (PMID 34956436, PMID 37598753, PMID 36467075, PMID 36941490).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analyses in type 2 diabetes report significant reductions in fasting glucose, HbA1c, insulin resistance, and several lipid markers with berberine (PMID 34956436; PMID 36467075). Separate pooled analyses also show modest improvements in glycemic and insulin-related traits (PMID 37598753) and dyslipidemia endpoints (PMID 36941490). The evidence is favorable overall, but most trials remain short and geographically concentrated.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports the L. reuteri Prodentis strain blend as an adjunct to nonsurgical periodontal therapy, but the clinical base is still composed mainly of small trials and short follow-up.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Key Prodentis-specific randomized trials include PMID 21523225 and PMID 23176716. Meta-analytic and systematic-review support for adjunctive L. reuteri in periodontal therapy includes PMID 32373987, PMID 37374951, and the newer umbrella synthesis PMID 40576216. The overall signal is positive but still strain- and protocol-specific.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded arginine-silicate complex with small randomized studies supporting acute vasodilation and executive-function benefits, but the evidence base remains modest and company-linked.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Kalman et al. (2016, PMID 27869715) reported faster Trail Making Test performance after 3 and 14 days of Nitrosigine supplementation in crossover trials. Rogers et al. (2020, PMID 32093766) found acute vasodilation effects comparable to citrulline malate, and Gills et al. (2021, PMID 34959823) reported short-term cognitive benefits in healthy adults.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports standardized water extracts of Eurycoma longifolia for sexual-health and testosterone-related outcomes, but the clearest PubMed-indexed trials are on Physta or generic standardized extracts rather than explicitly on LJ100.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Ismail et al. (2012, PMID 23243445) randomized men to a standardized water extract of Eurycoma longifolia (Physta) and reported improved erectile-function and sexual-well-being outcomes. Later randomized placebo-controlled trials include PMID 24550993 and PMID 34262417. The broader tongkat-ali literature is supportive, but the directly indexed branded evidence is not specifically LJ100-labeled.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: High-withanolide ashwagandha extract with multiple PubMed-indexed randomized trials for stress and sleep outcomes at relatively low doses.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Langade et al. (2020, PMID 32540634) randomized healthy adults with nonrestorative sleep and found better sleep quality and actigraphy outcomes with Shoden 120 mg daily. Lopresti et al. (2019, PMID 31517876) reported reduced stress and cortisol with Shoden 240 mg, and Mishra et al. (2024, PMID 39286132) found lower anxiety and cortisol plus higher testosterone at 60 mg and 120 mg doses.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Fenugreek extract with PubMed-indexed randomized evidence for libido, androgen-deficiency symptoms, and selected testosterone-related outcomes in men.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Steels et al. (2011, PMID 21312304) randomized healthy men and found improved libido with 600 mg/day of Testofen for 6 weeks. Rao et al. (2016, PMID 26791805) reported improved symptoms of androgen deficiency, sexual function, and testosterone measures after 12 weeks in ageing men.
