backed_clinical_studies.notes: Magnesium L-threonate formulation with randomized human evidence for cognition and emerging placebo-controlled evidence for sleep quality.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Zhang et al. (2022, PMID 36558392) found improved memory-test performance after 30 days of a Magtein-based formula in healthy adults. Lopresti and Smith (2025, PMID 41601871) reported better cognitive performance and sleep outcomes after 6 weeks of Magtein, and the 21-day sleep-focused trial is indexed as PMID 40567408.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports partially hydrolyzed guar gum for IBS-type symptoms, stool normalization, and microbiome effects, but the indexed trials are on PHGG as an ingredient rather than explicitly on the Sunfiber retail brand.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Niv et al. (2016, PMID 26855665) randomized adults with IBS and found improved bloating outcomes with PHGG versus placebo. Takahashi et al. (2019, PMID 31509971) reported better stool form and more Bifidobacterium after 3 months of PHGG. These findings support PHGG ingredient use, while the branded Sunfiber linkage is inferential rather than explicitly trial-labeled.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: One clear randomized placebo-controlled trial supports this solubilized keratin blend for hair and nail outcomes, but the formulation also contained vitamins and minerals so the keratin-specific contribution is not fully isolated.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Beer et al. (2014, PMID 25386609) randomized 50 women and found improved hair shedding, growth rate, strength, luster, nail hardness, and skin appearance after 90 days of Cynatine HNS 500 mg daily. This remains the main PubMed-indexed branded trial and includes co-administered micronutrients.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded tamarind-turmeric composition with multiple human randomized trials in knee discomfort and osteoarthritis, including early symptom improvement within days.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Arjmandi et al. (2019, PMID 31337958) reported improved six-minute walk distance and knee-comfort outcomes in non-arthritic adults taking TamaFlex. Kare et al. (2022, PMID 35096104) and Prasad et al. (2023, PMID 37351019) found reduced WOMAC scores and better joint function in knee-osteoarthritis cohorts using NXT15906F6 (TamaFlex).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports HMB for muscle preservation and recovery, with the clearest benefits in older adults, catabolic settings, and some resistance-training contexts rather than a universal ergogenic effect.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Wilson et al. (2014, PMID 24599749) found improved lean-mass and strength outcomes with HMB free acid in resistance-trained adults. Meta-analyses including PMID 35911112 and PMID 41305674 support HMB for muscle-related outcomes in older adults, while PMID 25700845 reported reduced abdominal adiposity when HMB was combined with resistance training.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Branded mango-leaf extract with crossover trials showing acute cognitive and exercise-performance effects; much of the sports literature uses Zynamite in combination with other flavonoids.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Wightman et al. (2020, PMID 32717999) found acute cognitive benefits and less mental fatigue after a single 300 mg Zynamite dose in healthy adults. Exercise studies include Gelabert-Rebato et al. (2019, PMID 30736383) and the single-dose Zynamite-plus-quercetin sprint trial (PMID 31661850).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Strain-specific human evidence supports HEAL9 most clearly for common-cold outcomes and, more recently, for cognition in moderately stressed adults; earlier wording overstated sleep-specific support.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Berggren et al. (2011, PMID 20803023) studied L. plantarum HEAL9 plus L. paracasei 8700:2 and found fewer and shorter common-cold episodes. Onning et al. (2023, PMID 37571403) reported improved cognition with isolated Lactiplantibacillus plantarum HEAL9 in moderately stressed adults.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports lutein, zeaxanthin, and meso-zeaxanthin mixtures for visual performance and screen-related eye-strain outcomes, but much of the indexed literature is ingredient-form rather than clearly brand-labeled Lutemax 2020.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Stringham et al. (2017, PMID 28661438) reported better visual performance, sleep quality, and reduced physical symptoms in high-screen-time adults taking a lutein-zeaxanthin-meso-zeaxanthin formulation. Additional human supplementation evidence includes Bovier et al. (2014, PMID 25251377) and Stringham et al. (2024, PMID 38890092), which support the carotenoid combination more broadly than a uniquely branded Lutemax 2020 trial set.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Purified shilajit ingredient with one clear randomized placebo-controlled trial in healthy middle-aged men and supportive older fertility literature on processed shilajit.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Pandit et al. (2016, PMID 26395129) randomized healthy volunteers and found higher total and free testosterone after 90 days of purified shilajit. Supportive older fertility data include processed shilajit in oligospermia (PMID 20078516), though that study is not clearly PrimaVie-branded.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Comparative human pharmacokinetic evidence suggests faster uptake and higher short-term serum retention than some standard vitamin C forms, but the evidence base is still small and focused on surrogate biomarkers.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Moyad et al. (2008, PMID 18971870) reported higher short-term serum vitamin C levels and lower oxidized LDL and CRP after PureWay-C versus comparator vitamin C forms in healthy volunteers. Supporting mechanistic work includes the cellular uptake study indexed as PMID 17901843, but clinical endpoint data remain limited.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Consistent evidence for mild-to-moderate depression contexts, but clinically significant CYP/P-gp drug interaction risk requires caution.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Cochrane review (CD000448) reports superiority to placebo in mild-to-moderate depression in many trials with better tolerability than some antidepressants.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Clinical data are broad and endpoint-specific; strongest support is for antioxidant biology rather than universal disease-risk reduction.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS and pooled human trials support antioxidant and deficiency-correction roles with context-dependent clinical outcomes.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for vitamin A and beta-carotene supplementation is context-specific. The strongest supplement-level evidence is in targeted eye-health formulations for selected AMD populations and in correction of deficiency states, not blanket supplementation for the general population.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: AREDS2 is indexed as PMID 23644932, with longer-term follow-up at PMID 35653117. Cochrane review PMID 37702300 supports antioxidant-vitamin/mineral formulas for selected AMD populations. NIH ODS also notes that provitamin A carotenoids such as beta-carotene do not share the teratogenic upper-limit concerns of preformed vitamin A.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports magnesium supplementation for selected outcomes such as blood pressure and migraine prevention, especially in deficiency-risk or higher-dose contexts. NIH ODS also notes that many Americans underconsume magnesium and that supplemental effects are outcome-, dose-, and population-dependent rather than universal.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Magnesium identifies hypertension, migraine, and low intake as the main clinically relevant contexts for supplementation. A recent blood-pressure meta-analysis found reductions of about 2.8/2.0 mm Hg overall, with larger effects in hypertensive or hypomagnesemic participants (PMID 41000008). For migraine, oral/intravenous magnesium meta-analysis and classic oral prophylaxis data support benefit in at least some patients (PMID 26752497; PMID 8792038).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Broad human evidence base in bone-health settings, especially when paired with vitamin D and adequate baseline intake assessment.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS and pooled trials support BMD outcomes, with strongest utility in at-risk populations and adequate adherence.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for vitamin B6 is indication-specific. The clearest supplementation support is for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and for some premenstrual symptoms, while broader neurological or cardiovascular claims are usually tied to deficiency correction or combination B-vitamin regimens. NIH ODS also emphasizes neuropathy risk at high chronic doses.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Vitamin B6 summarizes B6's established role in deficiency prevention and cautions against chronic high-dose exposure. A systematic review and meta-analysis found pyridoxine improves nausea-and-vomiting-of-pregnancy scores (PMID 36719452). Older randomized evidence also supports benefit for premenstrual symptoms, though trial quality was limited (PMID 10334745).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for thiamine is strongest in deficiency correction and in benfotiamine studies for diabetic neuropathy-related symptoms. The neuropathy literature is mixed: some shorter trials reported symptom benefit, but larger or longer placebo-controlled studies have shown smaller or null effects on objective neuropathy measures. NIH ODS still clearly supports thiamine's essentiality and deficiency-treatment role.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Thiamin anchors the evidence base around essentiality and deficiency correction. In diabetic neuropathy, early benfotiamine trials reported symptom benefit over 3 weeks and in the BENDIP study (PMID 15726875; PMID 18473286), but longer or more targeted placebo-controlled studies have shown null results on several objective endpoints despite improved thiamine status (PMID 22446172; PMID 41571333).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for vitamin B5 is limited compared with other vitamins. NIH ODS mainly supports pantothenic acid as an essential coenzyme-A precursor, while interventional supplement evidence is more specific to pantethine derivatives and modest lipid effects rather than broad clinical benefits.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Pantothenic Acid anchors the evidence around essentiality, coenzyme A synthesis, and deficiency prevention. For supplemental pantethine, randomized placebo-controlled trials reported modest LDL and total-cholesterol reductions in low-to-moderate cardiovascular-risk adults (PMID 21925346; PMID 24600231), but these data should not be overgeneralized to generic vitamin B5 supplementation.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Best human evidence for riboflavin is in migraine prevention, where high-dose protocols have repeated randomized support and recent meta-analyses. NIH ODS also supports riboflavin's essentiality and deficiency-correction role, but the supplementation signal is most specific to migraine rather than general neurological enhancement.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Riboflavin covers the vitamin's essentiality and deficiency treatment. In migraine, the original placebo-controlled 400 mg/day trial showed superiority over placebo (PMID 9484373), and later pooled analyses confirmed reductions in migraine frequency, duration, and pain scores (PMID 33779525; PMID 41769676).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human intervention data support potassium for blood-pressure reduction, especially in low-intake, high-sodium, or hypertensive contexts. NIH ODS also identifies potassium as a nutrient of public-health concern because intake is often below recommended levels, while noting supplementation safety limits in renal disease and with interacting drugs.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Potassium summarizes the mineral's role in blood-pressure regulation and the risks of excess supplementation in susceptible patients. Meta-analyses of randomized trials consistently report blood-pressure lowering with potassium supplementation, including PMIDs 1649867, 26039623, 28024910, 32500831, and the newer dose-response update PMID 40612568.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human supplementation evidence is strongest in iodine-deficient or marginally sufficient populations, including randomized trials showing thyroid and cognitive benefits when deficiency is corrected; excess iodine risk remains important.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Randomized supplementation trials in marginal iodine deficiency report thyroid and cognitive benefits (PMID 26891118; PMID 26203098). NIH ODS supports iodine's role in thyroid sufficiency and developmental health when baseline intake is low.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human supplementation evidence for manganese is limited. NIH ODS mainly supports manganese as an essential trace-element cofactor, while direct interventional outcome data are sparse and usually embedded in multimineral or deficiency-related contexts rather than stand-alone manganese efficacy trials.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Manganese anchors the evidence around enzyme cofactor biology, dietary adequacy, and toxicity risk from excess exposure. Because stand-alone randomized manganese supplementation trials with clear clinical endpoints are scarce, this entry should remain conservative and deficiency-oriented rather than claim broad therapeutic efficacy.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports copper as an essential cofactor with clinically meaningful effects in deficiency states.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS references essentiality and deficiency manifestations; supplementation should avoid excess exposure.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Evidence for phosphorus is primarily about essentiality, bone mineralization, and energy metabolism rather than supplement-specific performance or health benefits. NIH ODS indicates that frank deficiency is uncommon and that targeted supplementation is generally reserved for clinical or low-intake contexts.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: NIH ODS Phosphorus supports phosphorus as an essential mineral for bone and ATP-related metabolism while emphasizing that routine supplementation is rarely necessary in the general population. This entry therefore remains conservative and physiologic rather than intervention-driven.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Strong evidence base for LDL-lowering and bowel-regularity outcomes; supports cardiometabolic and GI-health use.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: FDA-authorized CHD claim for soluble fiber from psyllium (21 CFR 101.81) and multiple meta-analyses showing LDL reduction.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for boron is still small and biomarker-focused. Some trials of calcium fructoborate report short-term improvements in inflammatory markers and knee-discomfort scores, while older boron-supplementation studies found no clear anabolic or testosterone benefit in bodybuilders (PMID 25433580, PMID 24940052, PMID 8508192, PMID 7889885).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Calcium fructoborate placebo-controlled trials reported reductions in CRP, IL-6, lipids, and selected discomfort scores over 14 to 30 days (PMID 25433580; PMID 24940052). By contrast, earlier boron-supplementation trials in male bodybuilders did not show clear strength or testosterone benefits despite higher plasma boron (PMID 8508192; PMID 7889885).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence indicates immunomodulatory effects, but heterogeneity and methodological variability are substantial.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review/meta-analysis of human studies found favorable immune-marker changes with high heterogeneity (PMID: 37952511).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic review evidence suggests modest pain/sinus-related benefits with generally acceptable safety.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review/meta-analysis of 54 studies found small but significant oral pain benefit and potential sinusitis utility (PMID: 37157782).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human trials and meta-analyses support oral-health applications, with results influenced by extract standardization.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent meta-analysis of RCTs found propolis mouthwash reduced plaque/gingival inflammation outcomes (PMID: 41003407).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Evidence suggests modest, formulation-dependent effects for common-cold outcomes; heterogeneity across preparations is substantial.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Cochrane review (CD000530) and later meta-analyses indicate mixed but signal-positive findings in selected products and regimens.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic-review-level evidence supports honey for acute upper-respiratory symptom relief, especially cough severity, cough frequency, and sleep disruption in children. The evidence is for short-term symptomatic relief and does not justify broader immune or anti-infective claims (PMID 32817011, PMID 22419319, PMID 22869830, PMID 36126325).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A BMJ systematic review and meta-analysis found honey superior to usual care for combined URTI symptoms, cough frequency, and cough severity (PMID 32817011). Cochrane review and pediatric randomized trials also support short-term nocturnal cough relief relative to no treatment or some comparators (PMID 22419319; PMID 22869830; PMID 36126325).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence for DIM remains early and indication-specific. Small randomized trials show that DIM can alter estrogen-metabolism biomarkers, but robust clinical outcome evidence is limited and some settings raise interaction concerns, such as reduced tamoxifen-metabolite levels during adjunctive use (PMID 22075942, PMID 28560655).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A randomized placebo-controlled trial in women with low-grade cervical cytological abnormalities did not show clear lesion-resolution benefit despite biologic rationale (PMID 22075942). In tamoxifen-treated breast-cancer patients, BR-DIM shifted estrogen-metabolism biomarkers and raised SHBG but also lowered tamoxifen metabolites, making the clinical interpretation mixed (PMID 28560655).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Generic shilajit evidence is weaker and less standardized than purified branded forms, but it is no longer fair to describe it as purely preclinical. Human studies of processed or purified shilajit report effects on testosterone and male-fertility outcomes, while composition variability and contamination risk remain major caveats for non-standardized products (PMID 26395129; PMID 20078516).
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Processed or purified shilajit has human clinical support in oligospermia (PMID 20078516) and in a placebo-controlled testosterone trial in healthy middle-aged men (PMID 26395129). These data support a cautious ingredient-human posture, but the evidence should be interpreted through the lens of product purification and standardization rather than assumed to apply to any generic shilajit resin.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human RCTs and meta-analyses support pain-reduction outcomes; effect sizes vary by pain indication and study quality.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: 2023 systematic review/meta-analysis of double-blind RCTs (PMID: 36986081) reported significant pain reduction versus comparators.

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Extensive human evidence supports creatine monohydrate for strength, lean mass, and high-intensity performance. Evidence is strongest when paired with resistance training and adequate dosing.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Recent 2024 meta-analyses show improved strength and body-composition outcomes when creatine is paired with resistance training (PMIDs: 39519498, 39074168).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence supports enteric-coated peppermint oil for IBS symptom relief. Benefits are formulation-dependent and strongest for GI-targeted products.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analyses and systematic reviews show peppermint oil improves IBS outcomes versus placebo in multiple RCTs (e.g., PMIDs: 24060759, 31470006).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: LGG is one of the most studied probiotic strains with strain-specific human outcomes, especially for antibiotic-associated diarrhea prevention contexts.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review/meta-analysis found LGG reduced risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in relevant populations (PMID: 24780641).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: BB-12 has multiple human strain-specific trials with strongest consistency in selected GI and immune outcomes; effects vary by population and protocol.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Clinical literature shows strain-specific BB-12 human outcomes with strongest support in selected pediatric GI settings and mixed effects in other indications (PMIDs: 31797399, 32059116).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: HN019 has human strain-specific evidence, particularly for bowel function and transit outcomes in constipation-prone groups.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analysis and randomized trials report HN019-related improvements in transit-time and bowel-function outcomes in constipation-prone subgroups (PMIDs: 23922468, 29227175).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human evidence is emerging and mixed; some trials show modest stress/sleep improvements, but effect certainty remains lower than top-tier ingredients.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review of placebo-controlled human trials found limited evidence for stress and very limited evidence for sleep benefits of oral GABA (PMID: 33041752).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Systematic-review-level evidence supports chamomile for selected sleep and anxiety outcomes, with moderate heterogeneity by population and preparation.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: A 2024 systematic review/meta-analysis reported sleep-related improvements in several clinical contexts, with heterogeneity across populations and preparations (PMID: 39106912).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Nitrate-rich beetroot has systematic-review evidence for small-to-moderate improvements in exercise performance and modest blood-pressure reductions.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Meta-analyses report small-to-moderate ergogenic and vascular effects from dietary nitrate/beetroot interventions in adults (PMIDs: 33554654, 34965876).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Human systematic reviews support protein supplementation, including whey-rich interventions, for improving lean mass and strength outcomes when paired with resistance training.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review/meta-regression in healthy adults found protein supplementation improved RET-induced gains in strength and fat-free mass (PMID: 28698222). Recent meta-analysis in older adults found strongest effects when combined with resistance training (PMID: 39303495).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Citicoline has human trial support for selected cognition outcomes, with stronger signals in older adults and impairment-related contexts; evidence quality remains mixed across populations.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Randomized placebo-controlled trial in healthy older adults showed memory-related benefits (PMID: 33978188). Systematic review/meta-analysis found positive cognitive effects but overall study quality was variable (PMID: 36678257).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: Alpha-GPC has multiple human trials and meta-analytic coverage, with strongest effects in cognitive-impairment and combination-therapy contexts; healthy-adult evidence is emerging.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Systematic review/meta-analysis on adult-onset cognitive dysfunctions reported significant benefits in cognition and function outcomes across included trials (PMID: 36683513).

key_endpoints: GI adverse effects during antibiotic therapy ↓
backed_clinical_studies.notes: Strain-level yeast probiotic evidence is strongest for prevention of antibiotic-associated diarrhea, with additional supportive evidence in selected GI contexts.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: Updated systematic review/meta-analysis across 21 randomized trials found reduced antibiotic-associated diarrhea risk with S. boulardii (PMID: 26216624).

backed_clinical_studies.notes: FDA OTC monograph topical antifungal (21 CFR 333.210). In vitro fungistatic against C. albicans at MIC 0.5-2 mM. McLain et al. (2000) showed dose-dependent inhibition of Candida morphogenesis. Limited oral-specific RCTs; most human evidence is topical. Oral supplement use extrapolated from in-vitro MIC and medium-chain fatty acid pharmacokinetics.
backed_clinical_studies.notable_studies: McLain N, Bhagwandin B (2000): 10-undecenoic acid inhibits C. albicans morphogenesis in vitro, MIC 0.5-2 mM (Bioorg Med Chem 8:2491, PMID 11403866). FDA 21 CFR 333.210 OTC monograph for topical antifungal at 10-25% concentration. No large oral-specific RCTs identified; clinical use supported by FDA monograph status and in-vitro evidence.
